---
title: The Netto Number
---

The Netto Number combines a risk budget chosen before an evaluation period
with the adverse path realized during that period. It asks a different question
from Sharpe, Sortino, or Calmar:

> How much profit did this strategy produce relative to both the risk capacity
> it was granted and the downside it actually used?

QRT implements the calculation as `q.stats.netto_number`. The function is a
small scalar primitive; the important work is defining its three inputs with a
consistent mandate, horizon, unit, and adverse-excursion convention.

::: {.callout-important title="Status of the metric"}
The Netto Number is a practitioner-designed framework associated with trader
and author John Netto. Its economic intuition is useful, but it is not a
canonical academic standard and there is no broad public evidence that it
universally predicts investment skill better than established measures. Use it
as an additional risk-budget-aware diagnostic, not as a replacement for return,
drawdown, tail-risk, turnover, and benchmark analysis.
:::

## Origin and development

John Netto developed the modern Netto Number within his broader
Unit-of-Risk (UoR) framework. His 2016 book
[*The Global Macro Edge: Maximizing Return Per Unit-of-Risk*](https://books.google.com/books?id=lriIvgAACAAJ)
frames investment performance around return earned under a predetermined risk
budget. A [chapter excerpt hosted by CQG](https://news.cqg.com/sites/default/files/docs/The%20Global%20Macro%20Edge%20Chapter%205%20-%20Pages%201%20-%206%20CQG%20Excerpt.pdf)
describes the Netto Number as the most important UoR ratio and links it to the
Risk Factor Compensation System presented later in the book.

The idea was subsequently discussed in public trading presentations and
interviews. A 2017
[MoneyShow presentation](https://www.moneyshow.com/video/10761/)
describes the measure as a way to compare returns by manager and by unit of
risk. In a separate
[Cannon Trading interview](https://www.cannontrading.com/traders-interview/john-netto),
Netto describes evaluating a strategy, index, portfolio, or trade relative to a
predetermined risk budget rather than only in nominal terms. These materials
also connect the ratio to manager incentive design.

That compensation use is the historical motivation, not a restriction on the
mathematics. The formula can be applied to any well-defined decision unit with
an ex-ante risk budget and a realized P&L path: a trade, strategy, portfolio,
model checkpoint, or reinforcement-learning episode.

The public evidence base is concentrated in Netto's own book, excerpts,
presentations, and interviews. The framework should therefore be described as
a proprietary or bespoke practitioner metric, not as a finance law or a
peer-reviewed theorem.

## Definition

Let:

- $P$ be net realized profit over the evaluation period.
- $U$ be Unit-of-Risk: a positive loss budget fixed before the period.
- $A$ be maximum adverse excursion, expressed as a non-negative loss magnitude.

The framework first defines a Risk Factor as the arithmetic mean of planned
risk and realized adversity:

$$
\operatorname{RF} = \frac{U + A}{2}.
$$

The Netto Number is profit divided by that Risk Factor:

$$
\operatorname{NN}
= \frac{P}{\operatorname{RF}}
= \frac{2P}{U + A}.
$$

A value of $1$ means profit equals the blended Risk Factor. A value above $1$
means profit exceeded it. Zero means the period broke even, and a negative
value means the strategy lost money. There is no universal threshold that
separates a good value from a bad one; interpretation depends on a consistently
defined mandate and peer group.

::: {.callout-warning title="Negative-profit inversion"}
The raw ratio is not a risk-monotonic optimization objective for losing
outcomes. Holding $P$ and $U$ fixed:

$$
\frac{\partial \operatorname{NN}}{\partial A}
= -\frac{2P}{(U+A)^2}.
$$

For $P>0$, more adversity lowers the score as intended. For $P<0$, the
derivative is positive: more adversity moves the negative score toward zero.
With $P=-0.10$ and $U=1$, increasing $A$ from $0.10$ to $0.90$ changes the
score from approximately $-0.182$ to $-0.105$. A maximizer would prefer the
worse path.

This is a mathematical property of the original formula, not an implementation
error in QRT. The value remains valid as descriptive reporting, but must not be
the sole reward, loss, or policy-ranking criterion when outcomes can be
negative.
:::

### Published-style example

For $P=\$100{,}000$, $U=\$1{,}000{,}000$, and $A=\$500{,}000$:

$$
\operatorname{RF}
= \frac{\$1{,}000{,}000 + \$500{,}000}{2}
= \$750{,}000,
$$

and:

$$
\operatorname{NN}
= \frac{\$100{,}000}{\$750{,}000}
\approx 0.1333.
$$

```python
import qrt as q

q.stats.netto_number(
    100_000,
    unit_of_risk=1_000_000,
    max_adverse_excursion=500_000,
)
```

The ratio is scale-invariant. Dividing every input by the same starting capital
produces the same result:

```python
q.stats.netto_number(
    0.10,
    unit_of_risk=1.00,
    max_adverse_excursion=0.50,
)
```

Both calls return approximately `0.1333`.

## Input contract in QRT

`q.stats.netto_number` deliberately accepts three explicit scalar inputs rather
than a return series:

```python
q.stats.netto_number(
    profit,
    unit_of_risk=unit_of_risk,
    max_adverse_excursion=max_adverse_excursion,
)
```

The contract is strict:

- `profit` must be finite and may be positive, zero, or negative.
- `unit_of_risk` must be finite and strictly positive.
- `max_adverse_excursion` must be finite and non-negative.
- All values must cover the same object and evaluation period.
- All values must use the same units.

Currency amounts and fractions of starting capital are both valid. Mixing them
is not. For example, a dollar profit cannot be combined with a percentage risk
budget.

The function is not automatically included in `q.stats.performance` or
`q.stats.metrics`. A return stream contains no authoritative record of the
risk budget committed before trading, so QRT will not infer one after the fact.

## Adverse excursion is not automatically max drawdown

The most important implementation choice is the meaning of $A$.

QRT's `q.stats.max_drawdown` is the largest peak-to-trough decline from a
running equity high. Public Netto material also uses language such as maximum
negative drawdown or adverse excursion, with examples centered on loss of
starting principal. Under a principal-relative convention, giving back profit
while remaining above starting equity does not increase $A$; under standard
maximum drawdown, it does.

Consider an equity path that rises from `1.00` to `1.30` and then falls to
`1.05`:

- Principal-relative adverse excursion is `0.00`; equity never fell below its
  starting value.
- Peak-to-trough maximum drawdown is about `0.1923`; equity fell roughly 19.23%
  from `1.30` to `1.05`.

Those conventions answer different questions and can produce materially
different Netto Numbers. QRT therefore requires the loss magnitude explicitly.

For a principal-relative convention on a periodic return stream:

```python
equity = (1.0 + returns).cumprod()
profit = equity.iloc[-1] - 1.0
principal_adverse_excursion = max(0.0, 1.0 - equity.min())

score = q.stats.netto_number(
    profit,
    unit_of_risk=0.10,
    max_adverse_excursion=principal_adverse_excursion,
)
```

If a research mandate intentionally defines adversity as standard maximum
drawdown, make that variant explicit:

```python
score = q.stats.netto_number(
    profit,
    unit_of_risk=0.10,
    max_adverse_excursion=abs(q.stats.max_drawdown(returns)),
)
```

Report this as a "peak-to-trough Netto variant" rather than silently implying
that it is the only definition.

## Where it can be applied

The metric is not inherently a single-asset or portfolio measure. Its scope is
determined by the scope of its inputs.

| Evaluation unit | Profit | Unit-of-Risk | Adverse excursion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single trade | Net trade P&L | Pre-trade loss budget | Trade MAE |
| Single-asset strategy | Strategy-period P&L | Strategy risk budget | Strategy equity adversity |
| Multi-asset portfolio | Portfolio P&L | Portfolio mandate budget | Portfolio equity adversity |
| Model checkpoint | Validation/test P&L | Fixed evaluation budget | Evaluation-path adversity |
| RL episode | Terminal account P&L | Episode loss budget | Episode account adversity |

A raw asset does not possess an intrinsic Unit-of-Risk. AAPL, gold, or a futures
contract has returns and drawdowns, but the investor, strategy, or mandate must
supply the risk budget. Consequently, comparisons are meaningful only when the
budgets were fixed using comparable governance.

## Quant-research adaptations

### Strategy and hyperparameter evaluation

The Netto Number can complement model diagnostics when every candidate is
evaluated on the same data split, horizon, execution model, and risk budget.
For profitable candidates it rewards profit while retaining information about
the capital flexibility granted before the run. It must not be the sole ranking
objective across losing candidates because of the negative-profit inversion.

A useful experiment table should retain the components, not only the ratio:

| Field | Why retain it |
|---|---|
| `profit` | Distinguishes genuine profit from denominator effects |
| `unit_of_risk` | Makes the ex-ante mandate auditable |
| `max_adverse_excursion` | Exposes realized path stress |
| `netto_number` | Provides the combined comparison score |
| `max_drawdown` | Preserves conventional peak-to-trough risk |
| `turnover` and costs | Detects impractical high-churn policies |
| tail metrics | Detects risks hidden by one worst-path statistic |

Do not optimize the raw ratio over many configurations and then report the best
value on the same sample. Prefer a monotonic objective or explicit constraints,
retain the Netto Number as a diagnostic, lock the policy and risk convention,
and report once on untouched test data.

### Reinforcement-learning evaluation

::: {.callout-important title="Should I use the Netto Number in RL?"}
**Yes, but do not use the original Netto Number as the agent's main reward.**

Use it to **measure and audit** an RL agent:

- calculate it after each episode as a performance diagnostic;
- compare profit with both the risk budget and the adversity experienced;
- monitor whether the agent used or breached its risk budget;
- report it alongside return, drawdown, tail loss, turnover, and costs.

Do not use it by itself to teach the agent or select the best checkpoint. During
a losing episode, a worse adverse excursion can produce a numerically higher
Netto Number. An optimizer could therefore learn the wrong preference.

For training, use a reward that always prefers more profit and less risk, such
as net log return with monotonic drawdown penalties, or maximize return subject
to explicit drawdown and tail-loss constraints. In short: **use the original
Netto Number to evaluate the agent, not as its primary learning signal.**
:::

For a financial Gymnasium environment, calculate the metric from account equity
rather than from the return of one quoted asset. Let $V_0$ be starting account
value, $V_T$ terminal value, and $V_t$ the account path. A normalized episode
definition is:

$$
P = \frac{V_T}{V_0} - 1,
\qquad
A = \max\left(0, 1 - \min_t \frac{V_t}{V_0}\right),
$$

with a fixed normalized budget $U$, such as `0.10` for 10% of starting capital.
The raw Netto Number then describes the episode under that account-level risk
mandate. Do not use it by itself to rank arbitrary episodes or checkpoints.

Use it for descriptive episode reporting alongside:

- terminal return and net P&L;
- risk-budget utilization and breach rate;
- peak-to-trough drawdown and drawdown duration;
- expected shortfall and worst-decile outcomes;
- turnover, costs, and exposure.

It is a poor direct per-step reward. The denominator changes only when a new
adverse extreme occurs, the full score is unknown until the episode ends, and
the negative-profit inversion compounds the usual ratio-reward incentives for
inactivity, early stopping, tiny exposure, or other reward hacking.

#### Experimental monotonic adaptation

A Netto-inspired terminal score can retain separate planned- and realized-risk
terms without dividing negative profit by risk. Let $p_T=V_T/V_0-1$, let $u$
be the normalized risk budget, and let $D_T$ be a chosen non-negative drawdown
measure. One candidate is:

$$
S_T = \log(1+p_T)
- \lambda_N \log\left(1 + \frac{u+D_T}{2}\right),
\qquad p_T>-1,\ \lambda_N>0.
$$

It is monotonic in the intended directions:

$$
\frac{\partial S_T}{\partial p_T}=\frac{1}{1+p_T}>0,
\qquad
\frac{\partial S_T}{\partial D_T}
=-\frac{\lambda_N}{2\left(1+(u+D_T)/2\right)}<0.
$$

This is a QRT research adaptation, not John Netto's formula and not currently
implemented as a public statistic. Its weighting, drawdown convention, and
out-of-sample value require empirical validation before it becomes an API.

For dense feedback, define running peak $H_t=\max_{s\le t}V_s$, current
drawdown $d_t=1-V_t/H_t$, and running maximum drawdown
$D_t=\max_{s\le t}d_s$. A candidate training reward is:

$$
r_t = \Delta\log V_t
- \lambda_1 d_t
- \lambda_2(D_t-D_{t-1})
+ \beta\,\mathbf{1}_{t=T}\tanh(S_T/\tau).
$$

If account equity $V_t$ already includes fees, spread, slippage, and financing,
do not subtract those costs again. If it is a gross equity process, subtract an
explicit cost term once. The terminal bonus should be treated as an ablation,
not assumed to improve learning. Log-return rewards also require $V_t>0$; the
environment must define liquidation and termination behavior before equity can
reach zero.

#### State augmentation

Drawdown and terminal trajectory scores are path-dependent. An observation
containing only market features and current positions does not make their
reward dynamics Markov. Include sufficient account state, for example:

$$
\left[
V_t/V_0,\ H_t/V_0,\ d_t,\ D_t,\ u,\
\operatorname{remaining\ episode\ time}
\right],
$$

alongside positions and market features. The budget need only be observed when
it varies across episodes. This state summarizes the history needed to update
the drawdown terms; more general non-Markovian objectives may require a
trajectory-aware method.

When comparing RL agents, keep episode length and market windows aligned. A
short policy should not appear superior merely because it had less time to
encounter adversity.

For outer-loop selection, aggregate the experimental monotonic score $S_T$, not
the raw Netto Number, across a fixed panel of market episodes and random seeds.
Report at least the median, lower quartile, and risk-budget breach rate. A mean
alone can be dominated by a few favorable trajectories. Keep a final market
window untouched by reward and hyperparameter selection.

#### Evaluation protocol

A useful ablation compares:

| Configuration | Training objective |
|---|---|
| Baseline | Net log return |
| Drawdown additive | Log return minus current/new-drawdown penalties |
| Original Netto | Exact terminal ratio, included to expose failure modes rather than as a production default |
| Monotonic adaptation | Dense reward plus bounded $S_T$ terminal bonus |
| Constrained | Return objective with drawdown and tail-loss constraints |

Evaluate every configuration with metrics not all directly optimized: net
return, maximum drawdown, drawdown duration, Sortino, Calmar, expected
shortfall, worst-decile return, turnover, costs, budget-breach probability,
regime stability, and seed stability. Explicitly probe late risk-taking during
losing episodes, inactivity, episode-boundary liquidation, leverage artifacts,
and frequent small gains paired with rare catastrophic losses.

### Risk constraints and budget consumption

The ratio is most informative when Unit-of-Risk is a real constraint rather
than a label added after observing performance. A trading environment can track
principal-relative budget utilization:

$$
b_t = \frac{\max\left(0, V_0 - \min_{\tau \le t} V_\tau\right)}{U_{\$}}.
$$

Here $U_{\$}$ is the dollar loss budget. The environment may terminate an
episode or force liquidation when $b_t \ge 1$. This operational rule makes the
ex-ante input auditable and prevents researchers from changing the denominator
after seeing the path.

A cleaner alternative to combining every preference in one scalar reward is a
constrained problem:

$$
\max_\pi\ \mathbb{E}_\pi\left[\log\frac{V_T}{V_0}\right]
$$

subject to, for example:

$$
\mathbb{E}_\pi[D_T] \le d_{\max},
\qquad
\operatorname{CVaR}_\alpha(-p_T) \le L_{\max}.
$$

This separates compound-wealth growth from drawdown and tail-loss limits. The
constraint estimator, confidence level, and enforcement algorithm still need
careful validation; writing a constraint does not make finite-sample risk
estimates reliable automatically.

## Comparison with familiar metrics

| Metric | Numerator | Risk denominator | Ex-ante input? | Main question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sharpe | Mean excess return | Return volatility | No | Return per unit of total variability |
| Sortino | Mean excess return | Downside deviation | No | Return per unit of harmful variability |
| Calmar | CAGR | Peak-to-trough max drawdown | No | Annualized growth per worst drawdown |
| Recovery factor | Total return | Peak-to-trough max drawdown | No | Total gain per worst drawdown |
| Netto Number | Net profit | Mean of risk budget and adverse excursion | Yes | Profit relative to granted and realized risk |

The ex-ante term is the distinctive feature. It is also the source of the
metric's main governance burden: a subjective or movable budget destroys
comparability.

## Limitations and failure modes

### The 50/50 weighting is a design choice

The arithmetic mean gives equal weight to Unit-of-Risk and realized adversity.
Public descriptions provide the rule, but not a general statistical derivation
showing that equal weights are optimal. Researchers should treat it as a stated
utility choice and test whether it adds out-of-sample information.

### The budget can be gamed

A budget chosen after the result, changed during the period, or defined
inconsistently across candidates makes the ratio arbitrary. Record the budget,
unit, horizon, adversity convention, and breach rule before evaluation begins.

### Horizon and path conventions matter

Scores from a week and a year are not directly comparable. Neither are scores
based on principal shortfall and peak-to-trough drawdown. Reset rules, capital
flows, leverage, fees, and whether unrealized P&L is marked to market must also
match.

### One path statistic cannot describe tail risk

Maximum adverse excursion records one extreme. It does not reveal loss
frequency, duration, liquidity, gaps, skew, or the distribution beyond the
observed path. Pair the score with expected shortfall, drawdown duration,
stress tests, and simulation where appropriate.

### Small denominators and inactive policies

A very small risk budget and near-zero adverse excursion can create a large
ratio from modest profit. In RL, an agent may also avoid trading to protect its
denominator. Always inspect absolute profit, exposure, turnover, and the two
denominator components.

### It is not independently validated as a universal ranking rule

The formula has clear economic intuition but a limited public empirical
literature. Before using it as a production objective, test whether it improves
future outcomes relative to simpler baselines such as total return under a hard
risk constraint, Calmar, or a multi-objective return/drawdown criterion.

## Recommended reporting standard

A reproducible Netto Number should be accompanied by:

1. Evaluation object and exact start/end timestamps.
2. Profit unit and treatment of fees, financing, and cash flows.
3. Unit-of-Risk value, when it was fixed, and who or what set it.
4. Adverse-excursion definition and whether P&L was marked to market.
5. Starting capital and normalization convention.
6. Budget-breach and episode-termination rules.
7. Profit, Unit-of-Risk, adverse excursion, Risk Factor, and final ratio.
8. Conventional return, drawdown, tail-risk, exposure, and turnover metrics.
9. Whether the ratio was descriptive only or used in any selection objective.

With those controls, the Netto Number is a useful bridge between performance
measurement and risk-budget governance. Without them, its apparent precision
can hide arbitrary inputs.

## Sources and further reading

- John Netto, [*The Global Macro Edge: Maximizing Return Per Unit-of-Risk*](https://books.google.com/books?id=lriIvgAACAAJ), 2016.
- CQG, [excerpt from Chapter 5 of *The Global Macro Edge*](https://news.cqg.com/sites/default/files/docs/The%20Global%20Macro%20Edge%20Chapter%205%20-%20Pages%201%20-%206%20CQG%20Excerpt.pdf).
- MoneyShow, ["John Netto, The Protean Trader, explains the Netto Number"](https://www.moneyshow.com/video/10761/), 2017.
- Cannon Trading, [interview with John Netto](https://www.cannontrading.com/traders-interview/john-netto), discussing predetermined risk budgets across trades, strategies, indices, and portfolios.
- Wang et al., ["DeepTrader: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach for Risk-Return Balanced Portfolio Management with Market Conditions Embedding"](https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v35i1.16144), 2021. The method includes negative maximum drawdown as a reward in one of its policy components.
- Gaon and Brafman, ["Reinforcement Learning with Non-Markovian Rewards"](https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v34i04.5814), 2020. This provides general background on RL when rewards depend on history rather than only the latest state and action.
- Coache and Jaimungal, ["Reinforcement Learning with Dynamic Convex Risk Measures"](https://doi.org/10.1111/mafi.12388), published online in 2023 and in *Mathematical Finance* in 2024. The paper develops time-consistent policy-gradient and actor-critic methods and includes financial applications.
